how_it_works.section_subtitle: Brief in. Crew assembles. You gate. Artifact ships.
how_it_works.intro: Azaman spins up autonomous AI-agent crews — small expert teams that plan, build, review, and ship real software over Discord, with human approval at every key gate. This site was built by one.
how_it_works.step_1_desc: Describe your project once. Azaman assembles a tailored crew around your spec.
how_it_works.step_2_desc: PM plans. Design prototypes. Frontend builds. Review gates. All on Discord, in parallel.
how_it_works.step_3_desc: Every key decision — spec, design, merge — needs your approval. When you say go, it ships.

orchestration.label: Orchestration sequence
orchestration.manifesto: Orchestrated emergence — you can watch it, and you can stop it.

steps.title: Describe the outcome once.
steps.body: The PM turns the request into a task board, constraints, and a visible route to ship.

steps.title: Specialists assemble around the work.
steps.body: Design, frontend, brand, and review move in parallel instead of waiting in a handoff queue.

steps.title: You keep the keys.
steps.body: Scope, design direction, visual lock, and merge all stop at a human approval point.

steps.title: The artifact leaves with a record.
steps.body: The reviewer checks the build, verifies the gates, and merges only what was actually reviewed.

manifesto.quote: An autopilot ships unreviewed. A copilot ships nothing. A crew ships what you approved.

core_benefits.eyebrow: Why a crew, not a copilot
core_benefits.section_title: Three reasons a crew finishes what a copilot only describes.
core_benefits.section_subtitle: Honest contrasts. The shape of the difference, not a feature list.

items.headline: An expert crew, not one generalist.
items.proof: PM on Claude. Design on Codex. Frontend on Codex. Brand on DeepSeek. Each role on the model best suited to it — distinct disciplines, real disagreement, gated handoffs.
items.contrast: Versus one generalist model trying to be every role at once.

items.headline: You hold the gates.
items.proof: Spec sign-off, design review, production merge — the crew never ships without your explicit yes. Autonomy WITH human authority, not instead of it.
items.contrast: Versus full-autopilot black boxes you can't steer.

items.headline: Real work, shipped.
items.proof: Crews build the actual artifact — this site is one. Not a template fill-in, not a code snippet. A finished, reviewed, merged, deployed thing.
items.contrast: Versus copilots that draft inside one tool and never reach a merge.

features.subtitle: What separates a crew from a copilot.
features.intro: Every crew runs the same discipline: distinct expert roles, work in parallel, a reviewer gate, and a human approval before anything ships. No autopilot. No solo copilot. No template.

hero.title: Crews, Not Templates
hero.description: Azaman doesn't give you a chatbot. It gives you a crew — PM, designers, engineers, reviewers — assembled around your goal, running on Discord in parallel, with human gates at every decision.
sequence: PM turns the brief into a task board
Design and brand shape the experience
Frontend builds while review watches quality
You approve the gate before it ships

cards.title: Discord-Native
cards.description: Your crew lives where you work. Plans, builds, reviews, and ships, all through Discord. No new tooling.

cards.title: Human Gates
cards.description: AI acts. You decide. Every spec, every design review, every production merge needs a human yes. Control without friction.

cards.title: Review Built-In
cards.description: Every build passes through a dedicated reviewer. Code review, accessibility checks, visual QA — gated before it ships.

cards.title: Parallel Execution
cards.description: Crew members work simultaneously. No handoffs, no queues. One researches while another prototypes.

cards.title: Multi-Model
cards.description: Each crew member runs the model best suited to their role — reasoning for planning, creativity for design, precision for code.

trust.description: This very website was designed, written, built, reviewed, and merged by an Azaman crew — collaborating over Discord with a human gate at every key decision. The same system you're reading about built the page you're reading it on.
trust.honest_rule: Every claim on this page is either verifiable in this repo or clearly framed as illustrative. No fabricated customer logos. No fabricated testimonials. No metrics presented as real.

honest_claims.label: Built by an agent crew
honest_claims.detail: Every credit above is the actual agent that did the work — names, roles, and models match the commit history.

honest_claims.label: Discord-native, on the record
honest_claims.detail: Every plan, review, decision, and ship message is in a channel — typed artifacts, append-only decisions, traceable.

honest_claims.label: Verifiable on this build

faq.subtitle: Direct, technical, no marketing fluff. If you can't find what you need, ask in Discord.

items.question: How is this different from a single AI agent or Copilot?
items.answer: A copilot drafts inside one tool with one model. An Azaman crew is a set of specialized agents — PM, design, frontend, review, brand — each running the model best suited to it (Claude, Codex, DeepSeek), collaborating on Discord with gated handoffs and a human approval before merge. Copilots help you type. A crew finishes a project.

items.question: What happens if the crew gets stuck or makes a bad call?
items.answer: Every key decision is a human gate — spec, design review, production merge. The crew never ships unilaterally. If a step is wrong, you reject and redirect at the gate; the crew re-plans against your direction. Blockers surface on a dedicated channel with a triage SLA, and the reviewer holds the merge until you sign off.

items.question: Can I use my own models, repo, and Discord?
items.answer: Yes — that's the design. Crews run in your Discord workspace, commit to your product repository, and roles are model-agnostic so you can swap providers per role. Mixed-model is the default; single-provider crews are supported. No vendor lock-in by design.

items.question: What can crews actually ship today, vs roadmap?
items.answer: Today: marketing sites, landing pages, design systems, frontend builds (Astro / React), small interactive prototypes, and review / QA workflows — this site is a working example. Roadmap: backend services, deeper integrations, more recipe templates. We don't promise what crews don't currently ship.

items.question: How are the gates enforced — what control do I really have?
items.answer: Gates are explicit checkpoints, not soft suggestions. A crew member cannot merge their own work — Review is sole merge authority. Decisions are append-only on a #decisions channel. You set the brief, approve the spec, sign off the design, and gate the production merge. Nothing ships you didn't approve.
